claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 13 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care

Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, could you please tell me how many children you're looking to claim Child Benefit for, and what are their names and ages?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! I'm looking to claim Child Benefit for my child Alex, who is 13 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Alex.

To continue assessing your eligibility, I have a few more questions:

Does Alex live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. Since Alex doesn't live with you, I need to understand the situation a bit more.

Could you tell me why Alex lives elsewhere? For example:
- Are they living with another family member or person?
- Are they in hospital?
- Are they in local authority care?
- Are they at a boarding school or residential college?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives with their other parent, my ex-partner.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for explaining. Since Alex lives with your ex-partner, I need to ask a couple more questions to determine eligibility.

Do you contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep? For example, do you pay maintenance or contribute towards their living costs at least equal to the Child Benefit amount (currently £25.60 per week for an only or eldest child)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do contribute towards Alex's upkeep. I help with things like clothes and other expenses.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I have a couple more questions:

Is your ex-partner (the parent Alex lives with) already claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, my ex-partner isn't claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I have one more important question:

Are you and Alex both living in the UK, and are you responsible for Alex (even though they live with your ex-partner)? Also, could you confirm whether Alex is under 16, or if they're 16-19 and in approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is 13, so under 16. However, I don't currently live in the UK - I'm living abroad at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that important information. Since you're living abroad, I need to understand your situation a bit more.

Could you tell me:

1. Which country are you currently living in?
2. Are you working abroad, and if so, is it for a UK-based employer, or are you a Crown servant (such as a diplomat or member of the armed forces posted overseas)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm living abroad, but I'm not working for a UK-based employer or as a Crown servant - I'm just living overseas.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Based on what you've told me, I now have enough information to assess your eligibility.

Unfortunately, based on the UK government's eligibility rules, you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. Here's why:

To qualify for Child Benefit, you normally need to:
1. **Live in the UK** - This is a key requirement
2. Be responsible for a child under 16 (or under 20 in approved education/training)

While there are some exceptions for people living abroad (such as Crown servants like diplomats or members of the armed forces posted overseas, or those working for UK-based employers in certain countries), you've mentioned that none of these exceptions apply to your situation.

Since you're living abroad and are not working for a UK-based employer or serving as a Crown servant, you don't meet the residency requirement for Child Benefit.

Additionally, since Alex lives with your ex-partner in the UK, they may be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex instead, as they would meet the residency requirement and have Alex living with them.

Let me provide my formal eligibility assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on my assessment, I'm afraid you are **not eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex.

**Summary:**

| Child | Eligible? | Reason |
|-------|-----------|--------|
| Alex | No | You do not meet the UK residency requirement |

**Key points:**
- To qualify for Child Benefit, you normally need to live in the UK
- Although you contribute towards Alex's upkeep, and Alex is under 16, you are living abroad
- The exceptions for living abroad (such as being a Crown servant or working for a UK-based employer) do not apply to your situation

**What you could consider:**
Your ex-partner, who lives in the UK with Alex, may be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex instead, as they would meet both the residency requirement and the requirement of living with the child.

I'm sorry I couldn't give you better news. If your circumstances change (for example, if you return to live in the UK), you may wish to revisit your eligibility at that time.

reasonings: Alex is 13 years old (under 16), which meets the child's age requirement. The claimant states they contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep, and no one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex. However, the claimant does not live in the UK - they are living abroad and are not a Crown servant, nor working for a UK-based employer. The eligibility rules state that you 'normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK.' The exceptions for living abroad (Crown servants, certain countries, UK-based employers) do not apply in this case. Therefore, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirement and is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit because they do not meet the UK residency requirement. While they contribute to their child Alex's upkeep, they are living abroad without any of the qualifying exceptions (not a Crown servant, not working for a UK-based employer). Alex's other parent, who lives in the UK with Alex, may be eligible to claim Child Benefit instead.

The eligibility rules state that you 'normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK.' The exceptions for living abroad (Crown servants, certain countries, UK-based employers) do not apply in this case. Therefore, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirement and is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

While they contribute to their child Alex's upkeep, they are living abroad without any of the qualifying exceptions (not a Crown servant, not working for a UK-based employer). Alex's other parent, who lives in the UK with Alex, may be eligible to claim Child Benefit instead.
